name: bracket-lib
description: Roguelike toolkit (formerly RLTK) — virtual terminals, pathfinding, FOV, RNG; the de-facto Rust roguelike engine

kind.comment: Code-first game library with a virtual-terminal render + input game loop (GameState trait, main_loop), specialized for roguelikes. No editor or scene model — classified framework per RESEARCH.md's genre-specialized-toolkit inclusion.

license.comment: MIT (SPDX MIT) per GitHub license metadata.

repository.comment: Repo now lives under the amethyst org (github.com/thebracket/bracket-lib redirects there).

documentation.comment: docs.rs API docs are the primary reference. The README also points to the published 'Hands-on Rust' book and a free multi-chapter 'Roguelike Tutorial - In Rust'.

first-release.comment: GitHub repo created Jul 2, 2019; the predecessor `rltk` crate was first published Dec 6, 2019, with the renamed `bracket-lib` crate following Feb 22, 2020.

latest-stable-version.comment: 0.8.7 is the max stable version on crates.io, published Oct 4, 2022. The repo has newer unreleased work (dependency/backend updates through Dec 2025) not yet cut as a crates.io release.

maintenance-status.comment: As of 2026-07-06: commits are sporadic — a long gap between Jan 2023 and Sep 2025, then dependency/backend update commits in Sep and Dec 2025 (last push 2025-12-05). No new crates.io release since Oct 2022. Alive but slow-moving; scored passively-maintained.

maturity.comment: Feature-settled toolkit in use since 2019 and the basis of a published book, but still pre-1.0 (0.8.x) with breaking changes across minor versions. Scored stable rather than mature given the pre-1.0 API and niche scope.

backing.value: Amethyst Foundation (originally by Herbert Wolverson / thebracket)
backing.comment: Created and long maintained by Herbert Wolverson (thebracket); the repository now sits under the amethyst GitHub organisation (Amethyst Foundation).

implementation.comment: Pure Rust core; renders via OpenGL (glutin/winit), an optional wgpu backend, or WebGL for WASM. An optional `curses` feature wraps pancurses (C), but the default and web/desktop-GL paths are pure Rust.

architecture.comment: Immediate-mode game loop: you implement a GameState trait whose tick() is called each frame and redraw virtual consoles from your own state. No built-in ECS or scene graph — tutorials commonly pair it with specs/legion, but that is user choice, not part of the library.

built-in-ecs.comment: No first-class ECS. The 'Roguelike Tutorial' and 'Hands-on Rust' book bring in an external ECS (specs/legion), but bracket-lib itself does not provide one.

scripting.comment: Gameplay logic is written in Rust; no embedded scripting layer.

data-driven-scenes.comment: No scene/prefab format. It can load REX Paint (.xp) console art files as assets, but game scenes/entities are built in code, not authored to a runtime-loaded scene format.

reflection-serialization.comment: No engine-provided runtime reflection or game-state serialization; users can add serde themselves.

hot-reload.comment: No code or asset hot-reloading in dev.

visual-editor.comment: Code-first library with no graphical editor.

live-inspector.comment: No runtime world/entity inspector, first-party or de-facto.

asset-pipeline.comment: Loads bitmap fonts and REX Paint art, and offers an embedded_resource macro to bake assets into the binary — beyond raw include_bytes! (rating 1) but synchronous, with no processing/reprocessing pipeline or hot reload (rating 3+ needs async loading; 5 a full import pipeline). Scored 2.

cli-project-tooling.comment: No dedicated CLI, cargo plugin, or official starter templates — plain cargo project.

debug-profiling-tools.comment: Exposes a built-in FPS/frame-time counter (ctx.fps / ctx.frame_time_ms) but no profiler integration (no Tracy / egui overlay / diagnostics subsystem).

ide-support.comment: Nothing beyond standard Rust tooling (rust-analyzer just works); no engine-specific IDE integration.

rendering-backend.comment: OpenGL (default, via glutin) on desktop; WebGL for WASM/web; an optional wgpu backend (Vulkan/Metal/WebGPU); plus crossterm and pancurses (curses) text-terminal backends, tagged custom. Selected via feature flags.

lighting.comment: No rendering-level lighting system. It ships field-of-view / visibility algorithms, but that is roguelike gameplay logic (which tiles are visible), not a graphics lighting technique.

custom-shaders.comment: Uses internal shaders to draw its virtual consoles but exposes no user-facing custom-shader authoring API.

sprites-tilemaps.comment: Virtual console tile grids are the core abstraction; supports code-page-437 and graphical tile sheets plus multi-tile sprite consoles. Tilemaps/sprites are first-party by design.

post-processing.comment: Ships a built-in retro CRT-style post-processing pass (scanlines + screen-burn) toggled via ctx.with_post_scanlines. There is no general HDR bloom/tonemapping/AA pipeline, so this is a narrow, genre-flavoured post effect.

text-rendering.comment: Bitmap-font glyph rendering on virtual terminals is the library's core purpose (fixed-grid consoles, code page 437 and custom font sheets). No vector/TTF layout engine.

physics.comment: No physics system; bring your own.

audio.comment: No audio subsystem; bring your own (e.g. rodio/kira).

input.comment: Keyboard and mouse handled first-party (ctx.key, mouse position, click state). No first-party gamepad or touch input.

animation.comment: No animation system. Frame-based sprite animation can be hand-rolled by swapping glyphs/tiles, but there is no skeletal/sprite-frame/tween/state-machine facility built in.

ui-system.comment: In-game UI is drawn manually to virtual consoles (boxes, bars, printed text helpers); no widget/retained UI framework.

windows.comment: Supported native desktop target via the OpenGL backend.

macos.comment: Supported native desktop target via the OpenGL/wgpu backend.

linux.comment: Supported native desktop target; a 2023 commit fixed a Wayland crash, so both X11 and Wayland work.

web-wasm.comment: Compiles to wasm32-unknown-unknown and runs in the browser via the WebGL backend (the roguelike tutorial ships playable web builds).

ios.comment: No documented, officially supported iOS target.

android.comment: No documented, officially supported Android target.

clean-build-time.comment: Not measured on a consistent reference machine this pass; prefer null over a guess per RESEARCH.md. Its default OpenGL/glutin stack is lighter than the full wgpu engines, but no reliable figure recorded.

incremental-build-time.comment: Not measured this pass; prefer null over a guess.

binary-size.comment: Not measured this pass; prefer null over a guess.

web-bundle-size.comment: Web-capable via the WebGL backend, but the WASM bundle size was not measured this pass; prefer null over a guess (not N/A, since it does target web).

runtime-overhead.comment: Not measured with a defined methodology this pass; prefer null over a guess. Expected low given the lightweight fixed-grid console renderer, but no measurement recorded.

github-stars.comment: Snapshot from the GitHub API on 2026-07-06.

crates-io-downloads.comment: All-time downloads of the bracket-lib crate as of 2026-07-06. The predecessor `rltk` crate adds a further ~173,900 downloads under the old name.

last-release.comment: 0.8.7 published Oct 4, 2022 on crates.io. The repository has later unreleased commits (through Dec 2025) but no newer crates.io release.

shipped-titles.comment: Primarily used for learning: it backs the published 'Hands-on Rust' book and the free 'Roguelike Tutorial - In Rust'. No widely-documented commercially released title relies on it; the author's own Nox Futura used RLTK but is not a released commercial game. Set null per RESEARCH.md rather than list learning material as shipped titles.

community-size.comment: Single-author origin (now under the Amethyst org); a modest contributor base with open issues/PRs but no large dedicated chat community of its own. Scored small.

plugin-asset-ecosystem.comment: Ships as a family of focused crates (bracket-color, bracket-geometry, bracket-noise, bracket-pathfinding, bracket-random), but there is no third-party plugin/asset registry around it.

learning-resources.comment: Strong for its niche: a dedicated published book ('Hands-on Rust', Pragmatic Bookshelf) plus a comprehensive free multi-chapter 'Roguelike Tutorial - In Rust' and bundled examples. Short of 5 (no official mdBook for the library itself and little video ecosystem). Scored 4.
